name: Cerro de San Agustín by the Laguna de Almodóvar — circular from Puertollano
name_original: Cerro de San Agustín por la Laguna de Almodóvar — circular desde Puertollano
region_intro: Jogging routes around Puertollano traverse a diverse landscape, blending industrial heritage with natural beauty. The region features volcanic terrain, including maar and Strombolian volcanoes, alongside river valleys and small mountains adjacent to the Calatrava Massif. These geological formations and topographical features provide varied terrain for running, from flatter river paths to more challenging mountainous routes. Parks like Parque Pozo Norte and Parque de las Pocitas offer well-maintained spaces for leisurely runs within the city. Best jogging routes around Puertollano The most popular jogging route is Cerro de San Agustín by the Laguna de Almodóvar — circular from Puertollano, a 9.7 miles (15.6 km) difficult trail that takes 1 hour 50 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the volcanic landscape near the Laguna de Almodóvar. Another top favourite among local runners is Puertollano loop from Puertollano, a moderate 8.0 miles (12.9 km) path. This route provides an accessible run through the urban surroundings of Puertollano. Local runners also love the Collado de las Vacas loop from Dehesa Boyal Puertollano, a 7.5 miles (12.0 km) trail leading through the Dehesa Boyal area, often completed in about 1 hour 38 minutes. Jogging around Puertollano is defined by volcanic terrain, river valleys, and small mountains, with options for different ability levels. The network offers routes ranging from moderate to difficult, catering to various fitness levels. The routes in Puertollano are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 300 runners have used komoot to explore Puertollano's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Puertollano?
A: Komoot offers over 50 diverse running routes around Puertollano. These routes cater to various preferences, from moderate urban paths to more challenging trails through natural landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running routes in Puertollano?
A: The running routes in Puertollano feature a varied landscape. You can expect to encounter unique volcanic terrain, including features like the Maar and Strombolian volcano of Almodóvar del Campo, as well as river valleys along the Ojailén river and small mountains adjacent to the Calatrava Massif. This provides a mix of flatter paths and more challenging, undulating routes.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier run?
A: While many routes in Puertollano are rated moderate to difficult, there are options for easier runs, particularly within the city's parks. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129306" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puertollano loop from Puertollano&lt;/a&gt; offers an accessible, moderate run through urban surroundings, suitable for those looking for a less strenuous experience.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while jogging in Puertollano?
A: Puertollano's routes offer glimpses of its unique natural and historical landscape. You might run past the fascinating volcanic terrain, including the Maar and Strombolian volcano of Almodóvar del Campo (&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6295627" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;highlight&lt;/a&gt;), or the paleontological site of Monumento Natural Carbonífero. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7199638" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente Agria&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7286202" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Blanca&lt;/a&gt; are also notable natural attractions. For panoramic views, consider routes that pass by the Monument to the Miner on Cerro de Santa Ana.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the area?
A: Yes, several popular running routes in Puertollano are circular. A notable example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1210094153/cerro-de-san-agustin-by-the-laguna-de-almodovar-circular-from-puertollano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Agustín by the Laguna de Almodóvar — circular from Puertollano&lt;/a&gt;, which offers views of the volcanic landscape. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado de las Vacas loop from Dehesa Boyal Puertollano&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Puertollano?
A: The running routes in Puertollano are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Runners often appreciate the varied terrain, which combines urban accessibility with unique natural features like volcanic landscapes and river valleys, offering diverse experiences for all fitness levels.

Q: Are there any parks in Puertollano suitable for a leisurely run or family jogging?
A: Absolutely. Parks like Parque Pozo Norte (covering 38.2 acres) and Parque de las Pocitas (8.4 acres) are excellent choices for leisurely runs, walks, and family activities within the city. They offer well-maintained spaces and natural scenery.

Q: Can I find routes that offer good viewpoints or scenic vistas?
A: Yes, Puertollano offers routes with rewarding views. The Cerro de los Molinos, with its historic windmill ruins, provides scenic views. Additionally, the Monument to the Miner on Cerro de Santa Ana offers fantastic panoramic views of the city and its surroundings, making it a great destination for a run.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Puertollano?
A: Puertollano, located in Castilla-La Mancha, Spain, generally experiences hot summers and mild winters. Spring and autumn typically offer the most pleasant temperatures for running, with comfortable conditions for exploring both urban and natural trails. Early mornings or late evenings are advisable during the hotter months.

Q: Are there any historical sites or castles near the running trails?
A: Yes, the region around Puertollano is rich in history. While jogging, you might encounter views of historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2713619" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Calatrava la Nueva and Sacred Convent&lt;/a&gt;. The Cerro de los Molinos also holds historical significance with its 18th-century windmill ruins and former Islamic fortress site.

Q: What are some of the longer or more challenging running routes in Puertollano?
A: For runners seeking a greater challenge, Puertollano offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24529462" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Hinojosas de Calatrava – The Rollers loop from Umbría del Puerto&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 20 km route with significant elevation gain. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Dehesa Boyal Puertollano&lt;/a&gt;, which also features considerable elevation changes.
tips: It's not really an official viewpoint but it does have good views and is worth a visit if you're into these trails. The climb is not easy but it is doable and it is a good climb on a mountain bike ride.

In the Alcudia Valley, we find the municipality of Almodóvar del Campo built on the side of a volcano, so present in Campo de Calatrava, and whose crater is now a lagoon. The Arab footprint is the most emblematic of the city, leaving behind the memory of the Castle and above all the unique Parish of Our Lady of the Assumption. There you can see the first videomapping in Spain permanently installed in a closed space, under the theme of the life and work of San Juan de Ávila.

The Almodóvar del Campo Lagoon is a unique natural and geological feature located in the province of Ciudad Real, within the Campo de Calatrava region, famous for its volcanic landscapes.

It is a lagoon formed in the crater of a volcano after a phreatomagmatic eruption (interaction between magma and groundwater). This type of lagoon is called a maar.

waypoints.name: View from Cerro de San Agustín
